In September 2024, a sergeant with the St. Croix Tribal Police was on his way to respond to an alarm call in Webster, Wisconsin. While driving, he noticed a vehicle pulled over on the shoulder with its hazard lights flashing. It didn't seem urgent at the time, so he continued on to the call. After clearing the alarm without issue, he headed back the same way and saw that the vehicle was still there, flashers still going. Wanting to make sure everything was okay, he decided to stop and take a closer look. That's when he found a 29-year-old woman in the driver's seat, slumped over, setting the stage for a situation that quickly escalated beyond a simple welfare check.

As a result, the 29-year-old woman was charged with felony, battery threat to an officer, vehicle fleeing eluding an officer, failure to comply, misdemeanor, resisting obstructing an officer, resisting failing to stop, possession of THC, and possession of drug paraphernalia. She was eventually released on a $500 cash bond. Five months later, she pleaded guilty to resisting failing to stop. All the remaining charges were dismissed but read in. Ultimately, she was sentenced to pay $500 in fines, fees, and court costs.
